FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 9, 2007

Kenneth L. Glick
2897 Mareca Way
West Sacramento, CA 95691

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-004

Dear Mr. Glick:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See regulation 18329(b)(3)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Because your questions are general in nature and do not refer to any specific appearance before or communication with a state administrative agency, including your former agency employer, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. When will the one-year ban expire for your purposes?

2. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the California Resources Agency (the "CRA")?

3. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from participating in a presentation by the Northern Star Natural Gas Company (the "NSNG") before the state's informal Interagency Liquefied Natural Gas Working Group (the "Working Group")?

_______________

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 2

4. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the
Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (the "FERC") or the U.S. Maritime
Administration (the "USMA") in federal licensing proceedings?

5. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the
California State Lands Commission (the "CSLC"), the California Coastal Commission
(the "CCC"), or the California Public Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") with respect to
the Cabrillo Port, Oceanway, Clearwater, or Sound Energy Solutions projects?

6. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with state
agencies regarding proposed statutes or regulations of general applicability?

7. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the
California Energy Commission (the "CEC"), to assist the CEC in reassigning your prior
duties, or from communicating with the Attorney General's Office in regards to its
gasoline price investigation and legislative proposal pertaining to the sharing of
petroleum industry data?

8. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from representing other parties,
before federal and state agencies, in proceedings pertaining to the Frontier Project?

9. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from representing parties in
rulemaking proceedings before federal or state agencies concerning electric transmission
facilities?

10. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from requesting information
available to the public or from attending public education seminars, public workshops, or
public business meetings of the CEC?

CONCLUSIONS

1. For your purposes, the one-year ban will not expire until January 31, 2008.

2. The permanent ban prohibits you from communicating with any officer or
employee of any state administrative agency, including the CRA, for the purpose of
influencing any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding involving specific parties that
you previously participated in as a state employee. The one-year ban does not prohibit
you from communicating with the CRA if the work you performed, on behalf of the
CRA, was completed under the supervision of the CEC.

3. The permanent ban prohibits you from making a presentation before the
Working Group related to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding involving
specific parties that you previously participated in as a state employee. The one-year ban
also prohibits you from making a presentation before the Working Group if made for the

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 3

purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.

4. The permanent ban only prohibits your communications before the FERC or
the USMA, both federal agencies, if the communications are also made before a
California state administrative agency. Similarly, the one-year ban will only limit your
communications related to licensing proceedings before the FERC or the USMA if the
communications are also made before the CEC.

5. The permanent ban prohibits you from communicating with any officer or
employee of the CSLC, the CCC, or the CPUC for the purpose of influencing the
Cabrillo Port, Oceanway, Clearwater, and Sound Energy Solutions Projects if you
previously participated in the proceeding as a state employee. However, the one-year ban
does not apply to communications with the CSLC, the CCC, or the CPUC.

6. The permanent ban does not restrict communications related to proposed
statutes or regulations of general applicability. However, the one-year ban will apply to
any communications with the CEC pertaining to proposed statutes or regulations.

7. Neither the permanent ban nor the one-year ban restricts you from assisting the
CEC in redesigning your prior duties. Additionally, neither ban restricts your
communications with the Attorney General's Office regarding the office's legislative
proposal pertaining to the sharing of petroleum industry data. However, if the gasoline
price investigation by the Attorney General's Office involves specific parties, you may
not participate in the investigation if your communications with the office are made for
compensation and on behalf of any other person including, but not limited to, the NSNG.

8. From your account of the facts, you have participated in an informational
seminar and two informal staff meetings related to the Frontier Project. So long as you
have not participated in any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding involving
specific parties pertaining to the project, the permanent ban does not restrict your
communications related to the project. The one-year ban may apply, however, to any
communications related to the Frontier Project with the CEC.

9. The permanent ban will not restrict your communications related to
rulemaking proceedings concerning the implementation of the Energy Policy Act. The
one-year ban will prohibit you from communicating with the CEC to influence a
proceeding concerning the implementation of the Energy Policy Act, but it will not
restrict your communications with other state and federal agencies.

10. You may attend seminars, workshops, or meetings so long as they are general
informational meetings. You may also request any information concerning any matter of
public record from the CEC.

File No. 1-07-004
Page No. 4

FACTS

You left your position with the CEC on January 30, 2007. While you have not
performed any work for the CEC since January 19, 2007, you have elected to take
accrued vacation leave until your formal separation date of January 30, 2007. As an
employee of the CEC, you held the position of Senior Staff Counsel. This position is a
designated position within the CEC's conflict of interest code.

Organizationally, the CEC is within the CRA. However, the CEC has its own
budget, separately allocated by the Legislature, and is independently operated by its
executive director and appointed chairman and commissioners.³ The CEC does not have
any subordinate state agencies subject to its budgetary control.

As a Senior Staff Counsel for the CEC, you were generally required to offer legal
assistance to CEC staff involved in preparing technical reports on various energy
subjects; to provide legal and policy advice to senior staff, CEC Commissioners, and the
commissioner's advisors; and to present the policies and positions of the CEC in federal
and state regulatory proceedings. While working for the CEC, your work focused around
legal issues involving Liquefied Natural Gas ("LNG") and rulemaking and other
proceedings before the FERC.

Additionally, you served as the CEC's legal representative to the Working Group,
a voluntary collaboration of approximately 22 local, state, and federal agencies who share
an interest in the potential licensing of LNG terminals. Primarily, the Working Group
facilitates the exchange of LNG related information. The state agency members of the
Working Group are separately tasked to prepare recommendations to the Governor's
Office pertaining to the approval or rejection of the USMA licensing of LNG terminals in
the waters of the Outer Continental Shelf offshore California. The CEC's representatives
to the Working Group coordinate the Working Group's meeting agendas. The CEC's
legal representative(s) to the Working Group prepare and coordinate the recommendation
packages for the Governor's Office. These packages are subject to the review of the
Working Group's member agencies and the CRA.

In the course of your employment with the CEC you have participated in the
following proceedings:

• Sound Energy Solutions Project – Participated in licensing proceeding before the
FERC regarding a LNG terminal for the Port of Long Beach, California, including
the preparation of a safety advisory report.

³ The CEC's Chairman and Commissioners are appointed by the Governor. (Public Resources
Code sections 25200 and 25212.) The CEC Commissioners appoint the Executive Director. (Public
Resources Code section 25217.) The Secretary of the CRA and the President of the Public Utilities
Commission are nonvoting members of the CEC. (Public Resources Code section 25202.)

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 5

• Cabrillo Port Project – Participated in a licensing proceeding before the USMA
regarding a LNG terminal proposed offshore Ventura County, California.

• Participated in a long term procurement proceeding for the state's three investor-
owned electric utilities before the CPUC.

• Participated in various rulemaking proceedings before the U.S. Department of
Energy (the "USDE") and before the FERC regarding electric transmission
facilities.

• Participated in gasoline and diesel fuel pricing investigation. This investigation
culminated in several reports to the Governor's Office clarifying the CEC's data
collection authority and permitting data sharing with the Attorney General's
Office.

• Frontier Project – Attended a public seminar for four western states introducing a
potential interstate electric transmission line. You also participated in two
internal CEC staff meetings to collect data regarding the project.

• Worked in conjunction with the State Board of Equalization to design a fee
collection mechanism and to draft regulatory and legislative changes to assess
fees to proponents of LNG terminal.

• Provided legal advice, drafted pleadings, and filed legal documents with the
FERC and the USDE for the CRA under the direction of the Executive Director of
the CEC. All work was performed from your location with the CEC and you
were not formally loaned or posted to the CRA.

• Drafted legal pleadings filed with the FERC on behalf of the National Association
of Regulatory Utility Commissioners and the Western Interstate Energy Board/
Committee on Regional Electric Power Cooperation.

• Prepared draft recommendation packages, as a member of the Working Group, for
the Governor's Office pertaining to the BHP-Billiton LNG federal license
proceeding.

• Provided analyses for legislative proposals addressing LNG and electricity issues.

• The Oceanway and the Clearwater Projects – While you were not assigned to
these projects, you requested a copy of a safety study from the sponsor of the
Oceanway Project as a CEC employee. You have also attended two meetings of

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 6

the Working Group at which the sponsoring companies of these projects,
respectively, Woodside Natural Gas and the NSNG, introduced these projects.

After leaving state service you will be employed by the NSNG. The NSNG has
filed an application before the FERC and NOAA for a license permitting it to construct and operate
an LNG terminal in the waters of Outer Continental Shelf offshore California (the
"Clearwater Project"). The NSNG has also filed an application for a license permitting it
to construct and operate an LNG terminal in the Columbia River, Oregon (the "Oregon
Project"). You will be employed by the NSNG within its governmental, regulatory, and
public affairs department and will be assigned to support the company's applications for
these two projects.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-
governmental employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the
"revolving door" prohibitions.⁴ In addition, section 87407 prohibits certain state and
local officials from participating in decision making, or using their official position to
influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or
have any arrangement concerning employment. (Section 87407; regulation
18747.)⁵

• Permanent Ban: The first restriction is the "permanent ban" prohibiting a former
state employee from "switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any
specific proceeding involving the State of California or assisting others in the
proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the state (see sections 87401-87402, regulation
18741.1).

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applying to those officials who have
permanently left state service or have taken a leave of absence. (Regulation
18741.1(a)(1).) The permanent ban applies only when a former employee or official
receives compensation for making an appearance or communication, or for aiding,
advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing any other person, other than
the State of California, for the purpose of influencing a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(2) and (3).)

⁴ A comprehensive discussion of the elements of the two laws is set forth in the attached "Leaving
Your State Job? Post-Employment Restrictions May Affect You." We have enclosed this document for
your information. We do not repeat this information in the body of this letter.

⁵ According to the facts you have provided, you have already accepted employment with a private
sector company. Any advice issued at this time pertaining to section 87407 would relate to past conduct.
As the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct, we provide no opinion as to the
application of section 87407 to your particular circumstances.

File No. 1-07-004
Page No. 7


"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application,
request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in
any court or state administrative agency . . .'" (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an
official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . .'" (Section 87400(d); regulation 18741.1(a)(4).)

Finally, the permanent ban only applies to those judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceedings in which the official participated while employed by the state. (Regulation
18741.1(a)(5).) "The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases
where the proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the
official had participated. A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically
involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues from those
considered in previous proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; see also
Donovan Advice Letter, No. 1-03-119.)

• One-Year Ban: The second restriction is the "one-year ban" prohibiting a state
employee from making an appearance or communicating, for compensation,
before or with his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing certain
administrative or legislative action or influencing certain proceedings. (See
section 87406, regulation 18746.1.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who
holds a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict of
interest code. (Section 87406(d)(1); regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) For those subject to the
one-year ban, the ban will apply for 12 months from the date he or she leaves office or
employment, which is defined as the date the employee or official permanently leaves
state service or takes a leave of absence. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(1) and (2).)

While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official
is being compensated for his or her appearances or communications before his or her
former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that
person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to "judicial or quasi-judicial"
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation
18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is made for the "purpose of influencing" if it
is made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying,

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 8

opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An
appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or
in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting,
and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are before a
state agency the public official worked for or represented or a state agency which budget,
personnel, and other operations are subject to the control of a state agency the public
official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).)

While we have detailed the general post-governmental employment provisions for
your review, you have not sought advice pertaining to any particular appearance or
communication. You have, however, asked several more specific questions regarding the
application of the revolving door provisions, which we will discuss in turn.

1. Term of the One-Year Ban

Regulation 18746.1 provides that the one-year ban commences when an official
"has permanently left state service or is on a leave of absence." In the Weil Advice
Letter, No. A-97-247, we examined whether the one-year ban commences the day an
official physically stops working or on the official's formal separation date if the official
elects to use accrued vacation time to continue receiving regular paychecks beyond the
date on which the official physically stops working. Under the facts presented in the Weil
Letter, we concluded that the one-year period commences when the employee is no
longer being compensated, including compensation for accrued vacation time, and is no
longer under an employment agreement.

Likewise, you have elected to use your accrued vacation time to continue
receiving regular paychecks until January 30, 2007. Under the facts you have provided,
we conclude that the one-year ban will not expire until January 31, 2008.

2. Communications with the CRA

Permanent Ban - The permanent ban prohibits you from appearing before or
communicating with any officer or employee of any state administrative agency, in
representation of the NSNG, including the CRA, for the purpose of influencing any
judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding involving specific parties that you previously
participated in as a state employee. (Regulation 18741.1.)

In terms of NSNG projects, communications with the CRA will be made to
influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding involving a specific party, the
NSNG. Therefore, the permanent ban prohibits you from appearing before or
communicating with the CRA if you have previously participated in the proceeding.

File No. 1-07-004
Page No. 9

You have identified several judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings
involving specific parties that you have participated in to various degrees. Appearances
before or communications with the CRA to influence these proceedings would be
prohibited under the permanent ban. From your request for advice, your involvement in
the Clearwater Project is of particular concern. You have "participated" in a proceeding
if you took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial
basis, investigation, or use of confidential information . . . ." (Section 87400(d).)

From the facts you have provided, you were not specifically assigned to the
Clearwater Project. However, you did attend a Working Group meeting at which the
project was introduced and were your agency's legal representative to the Working
Group. Based upon the facts provided, we are unable to determine the extent of your
involvement with the Clearwater Project, and we must leave to you the final
determination of whether you previously participated in this project.

One-Year Ban - The one-year ban prohibits you from appearing before or
communicating with the state agency you worked for or represented or those state
agencies which budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the control of a
state agency you worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).) You have
provided that the CEC is within the CRA. As the CEC is within the CRA, the pertinent
question is whether the CRA is your former state administrative agency employer.

In determining an official's former state agency employer, we have advised:

"In identifying an official's 'state administrative agency,' within the
meaning of section 87406, we look to the administrative body that controls
the budget, personnel and other operations of the entity where a former
employee worked. It is this body, together with any agencies, boards,
commissions, or organizational units subject to its control, that are jointly
considered to be the ex-employee's former state administrative agency
employer." (Corum Advice Letter, No. A-02-258.)

While you have stated that the CEC is within the CRA, you have also stated that
the CEC has its own budget allocated by the Legislature and is independently operated by
its executive director and appointed chairman and commissioners. Based upon the facts
you have provided, the CEC, not the CRA, is your former state administrative employer.

Notwithstanding our determination that the CRA is not your former state
administrative employer, we must also determine whether you "worked for or
represented" the CRA considering the fact that you provided legal advice, drafted
pleadings, and filed legal documents for the CRA. In defining these terms, as used in
regulation 18746.1(b)(6), we have stated:

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 10

"'[W]orked for or represented' means the former official was employed
by, loaned to, or assigned to perform services for the state administrative
agency. If assigned but not loaned to an agency, a former official would
have 'worked for or represented' that agency only if he or she acted on
behalf of the agency and performed services under the supervision of that
agency." (Staff Memorandum to Commission, Adoption of Revolving
Door Regulations (Section 87406); Regulations 18746.1 and 18746.2,
dated October 26, 1998.)

Although you have stated that you provided legal advice, drafted pleadings, and
filed legal documents for the CRA, you have also stated that you did so from your
location with the CEC and under the direction of the CEC's executive director. Based
upon the facts you have provided, while it appears that you were assigned to perform
services for the CRA and that you acted on behalf of the CRA, you performed these
services under the supervision of the CEC and not the CRA.

Based upon the facts you have provided, the CRA is not your former state
administrative employer, and you have neither worked for nor represented the CRA as
defined by regulation 18746.1. Accordingly, we conclude that the one-year ban does not
prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with the CRA.

3. Participating in Presentations before the Working Group

Permanent Ban - The permanent ban prohibits you from appearing before or
communicating with any officer or employee of any state administrative agency, in
representation of the NSNG, for the purpose of influencing any judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceeding involving specific parties that you previously participated in as a state
employee. (Regulation 18741.1) Because the Working Group consist of multiple state
administrative agencies, appearances before or communications with the Working Group
will be restricted if made for the purpose of influencing judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceedings involving specific parties, in which you previously participated.

You have indicated that the members of the Working Group each prepare
recommendations for the Governor's Office pertaining to the licensing of LNG terminals.
Undoubtedly, presentations before the Working Group will influence recommendations
made to the Governor's Office. In terms of NSNG projects, presentations before the
Working Group will be made to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
involving as specific party, the NSNG. Therefore, the permanent ban prohibits you from
presenting NSNG projects before the Working Group if you have previously participated
in the proceeding.

As addressed above, you have identified several judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceedings involving specific parties that you have participated in to various degrees.
Accordingly, presentations made to the Working Group to influence these proceedings

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 11

would be prohibited under the permanent ban. In your request for advice, you are
particularly concerned with presentations before the Working Group related to the
Clearwater Project. As discussed above, we are unable to determine the extent of your
involvement with the Clearwater Project, and we must leave to you the final
determination of whether you previously participated in this project.6

One-Year Ban - The one-year ban will restrict you from appearing before or
communicating with any officer or employee of your former state agency for the purpose
of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Regulation
18746.1.) Because the CEC is a member of the Working Group your appearances before
or communications with the Working Group will be before an officer or employee of
your former state agency. Therefore, appearances before or communications with the
Working Group are prohibited if made for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property.

You have stated that members of the Working Group each make
recommendations to the Governor's Office pertaining to the licensing of LNG terminals.
Furthermore, you have stated that the Working Group facilitates the exchange of LNG
information. You have not, however, identified the scope of any presentations you may
make before the Working Group, and we do not have enough facts to determine whether
any particular presentation (other than a presentation related to the Clearwater Project)
would be prohibited under the one-year ban.

In terms of presentations related to the Clearwater Project, the CEC makes
recommendations to the Governor's Office pertaining to LNG projects and the
Governor's Office uses this recommendation, as well as the recommendations of other
agencies participating in the Working Group, to formulate the office's recommendations
under federal licensing procedures. Presentations before the Working Group could
influence the CEC's recommendation to the Governor' Office pertaining to the NISNG's
federal licensing of a LNG terminal. As this is a proceeding involving the issuance of a

6 We note that in your request for advice, you suggest that the Clearwater Project may be a new
proceeding from the proceeding you may have previously participated in, because the project has
significantly changed since it was introduced to the Working Group. You specifically stated that the
project had changed in terms of the facilities, services, and environmental mitigation. As we have
previously advised, "'new' proceeding typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or
different factual issues from those considered in previous proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, supra; see
also Donovan Advice Letter, No. supra.) However, despite the changes to the Clearwater Project, in terms
of the facilities, services, and environmental mitigation, the parties have not changed, and there are many similar factual issues. Accordingly, we conclude that the
Clearwater Project does not appear to be a new proceeding.

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 12

license, you are prohibited by the one-year ban from making presentations before the
Working Group related to the Clearwater Project.

4. Communications with the FERC and the USMA in Federal Licensing
Proceedings regarding the Sound Energy Solutions and the Cabrillo Port Projects

Permanent Ban - Federal licensing procedures are judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceedings involving specific parties. From the facts you have provided, you have
previously participated in the Sound Energy Solutions and the Cabrillo Port projects.
Thus, the permanent ban will apply if you make "an appearance or communication before
any officer or employee of any state administrative agency for the purpose of
influencing" the proceeding. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).)

Appearances or communications solely before the FERC and the USMA, both
federal agencies, are not prohibited under the permanent ban as you will not be making
the appearances or communications before any officer or employee of a California state
administrative agency. However, we must caution that appearances before or
communications with the FERC and the USMA in licensing proceedings may be
prohibited if the appearances or communications would also be before or with a
California state administrative agency. For example, if a FERC or USMA meeting is
attended by employees of a California state administrative agency, any appearances or
communications on your part are prohibited to the extent that these communications
involve "supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing" the proceeding. (Regulation 18746.2; see also Lucas Advice Letter, No. I-05-
160.)

One-Year Ban - The one-year ban will restrict you from appearing before or
communicating with any officer or employee of your former state agency for the purpose
of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Regulation
18746.1.)

From the facts provided the Sound Energy Solutions and the Cabrillo Port
projects are federal licensing proceedings and, thus, involve the issuance of a license.
Appearances or communications solely before the FERC and the USMA, both federal
agencies, are not prohibited under the one-year ban as you will not be making the
appearances or communications before any officer or employee of your former state
administrative agency. However, we must again caution that appearances before or
communications with the FERC and the USMA in licensing proceedings may be
prohibited if the appearances or communications would also be before or with the CEC,
such as a FERC or USMA meeting attended by CEC employees.

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 13

5. Communications with the CSLC, the CCC, or the CPUC related to the
Cabrillo Port, Oceanway, Clearwater, and Sound Energy Solutions Projects

Permanent Ban - As discussed above, the permanent ban will restrict your
appearances or communications, in representation of NSNG, before or with any officer or
employee of any state administrative agency for the purpose of influencing a judicial,
quasi-judicial, or any other proceeding involving specific parties that you previously
participated in. (Regulation 18741.1.) The CSLC, the CCC, and the CPUC are all state
administrative agencies. Moreover, the proceedings, as you have described them, related
to the Cabrillo Port, Oceanway, Clearwater, and Sound Energy Solutions projects were
judicial or quasi-judicial proceedings. Appearances or communications made to
influence these proceedings are prohibited by the permanent ban if you previously
participated in the proceedings as a state employee.

From the facts you have provided, you have previously participated in the
Cabrillo Port and the Sound Energy Solutions projects. Thus, you are prohibited from
appearing before or communicating with the CSLC, the CCC, and the CPUC to influence
these proceedings.7 However, the question of whether you may make appearances or
communications pertaining to the Oceanway or the Clearwater projects is not as easily
answered. From the facts provided, we are unable to determine the extent of your
involvement in the Oceanway or the Clearwater projects, and we must leave to you the
final determination of whether you previously participated in these projects.

One-Year Ban - As discussed above, the one-year ban only applies to
appearances or communications before the state agency you worked for or represented or
those state agencies which budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the
control of a state agency you worked for or represented. From the facts you have
provided, you have not worked for or represented the CSLC, the CCC, or the CPUC.
Furthermore, these three agencies are not within the CEC's budgetary or appointive
control. Accordingly, the one-year ban does not apply to your appearances before or
communications with the CSLC, the CCC, or the CPUC.

6. Communications related to Proposed Statutes or Regulations of General
Applicability such as a Fee Imposed on the Proponents of LNG Terminals

Permanent Ban - As discussed above the permanent ban only applies to judicial,
quasi-judicial, or other proceedings involving specific parties. The permanent ban does
not prohibit appearances or communications related to proposed statutes or regulations of
general applicability.

One-Year Ban - The one-year ban applies to any administrative or legislative
action including proposed statutes or regulations of general applicability. As discussed
above, the one-year ban will restrict your appearances before or communications with the
CEC pertaining to proposed statutes or regulations of general applicability for one-year.

7. Communications with the CEC to Assist the CEC in Reassigning Your Prior
Duties and Communications with the Attorney General's Office in regards to its Gasoline
Price Investigation and Legislative Proposal Pertaining to the Sharing of Petroleum
Industry Data

Permanent and One-Year Ban - The permanent ban and the one-year ban apply
only when the former official makes an appearance or communication on behalf of
another person and for compensation. (Regulations 18741.1(a)(2) and 18746.1(b)(3) and
(4).) Neither the permanent ban nor the one-year ban would prohibit you from appearing
before or communicating with the CEC to assist the CEC in reassigning your prior duties.

In terms of communications with the Attorney General's Office regarding its
legislative proposal pertaining to the sharing of petroleum industry data, the one-year ban
only applies to appearances or communications before the state agency you worked for or
represented or those state agencies which budget, personnel, and other operations are
subject to the control of a state agency you worked for or represented. From the facts
provided, you have neither worked for nor represented the Attorney General's Office, and
the office is not within the control of the CEC. Accordingly, the one-year ban would not
apply to your communications with the Attorney General's Office. Moreover, the
permanent ban only applies to judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings involving
specific parties. Because the legislative proposal is not a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding involving specific parties, the permanent ban would not restrict your
communications with the Attorney General's Office related to the legislative proposal.

In terms of communications with the Attorney General's Office related to its
gasoline price investigation, again, the one-year ban would not apply to these
communications. However, you have not provided us with sufficient facts to determine
whether this investigation involves specific parties. If the investigation involves specific
parties, you may participate in the investigation only if your communications with the
Attorney General's Office are not made for compensation and not made on behalf of any
other person including, but not limited to, the NSNG.

8. Communications Regarding the Frontier Project

Permanent Ban - You have stated that you attended a public seminar introducing
the Frontier Project. The seminar was sponsored by the governors of the four states
involved in the project. You have also stated that you attended two internal CEC staff
meetings held to gather data related to the project. As discussed above, the permanent
ban applies to judicial, quasi- judicial, or other proceedings involving specific parties.

File No. I-07-004
Page No. 15

From your account of the facts, the informational seminar and the internal staff meetings
were held to introduce the project and to gather information. So long as you have not
participated in any proceeding, application, request for a ruling, or other determination
concerning the matter involving a specific party or parties pertaining to the project, the permanent ban
does not prohibit your appearances or communications related to the project.

One-Year Ban – The one-year ban will apply to "any appearance or
communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) Accordingly, the one-year ban will restrict
appearances before or communications with the CEC related to the Frontier Project
unless the communications are made in a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
involving specific parties, which does not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property. Note, however, that the permanent ban may still apply to any judicial, quasi-
judicial, or other proceedings involving specific parties.

9. The Permanent Ban and Rulemaking Proceedings before Federal and State
Agencies Concerning the Implementation of the Energy Policy Act

Permanent Ban - Again, the permanent ban only applies to judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings involving specific parties. Therefore, the permanent ban
will not prohibit appearances or communications related to rulemaking proceedings
concerning the implementation of the Energy Policy Act.

One-Year Ban - The one-year ban only applies if you make an appearance before
or communication with your former state administrative agency employer. Accordingly,
the one-year ban will prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with the
CEC to influence a proceeding concerning the implementation of the Energy Policy Act.
However, appearances before or communications with state and federal agencies, other
than the CEC, are not prohibited under the one-year ban.

10. Appearing in the Audience at Public Education Seminars, Public Workshops,
or Public Business Meetings of the CEC and Communicating with the CEC to Request
Public Information

Permanent and One-Year Ban - The permanent ban and the one-year ban only
apply if the former official makes an appearance or communication "for the purpose of
influencing" as defined in regulation 18746.2. (Regulations 18741.1(a)(3) and
18746.1(b)(5).) Regulation 18746.2 provides the following in pertinent part:

"(b) An appearance or communication is not limited by this section when an
individual:

"(1) Participates as a panelists or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;

"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;

"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or

"(4) Communicates with the press."

Specifically, you ask whether you may attend public education seminars, public
workshops, or public business meetings of the CEC. Under regulation 18746.2(b)(2),
you may attend these seminars, workshops, or meetings only if they are general
informational meetings and so long as you do not make any other appearances or
communications attending the meetings, which would otherwise be prohibited by
either the permanent or one-year ban.

Moreover, you ask whether you may request public information from the CEC.
Under regulation 18746.2(b)(3), you may request any information concerning any matter
of public record. Consequently, you may request public information from the CEC.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Glick 2897 Mareca Way West Sacramento, CA 95691 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-07-004 Dear Mr. Glick: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See regulation 18329(b)(3)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature and do not refer to any specific appearance before or communication with a state administrative agency, including your former agency employer, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTIONS 1. When will the one-year ban expire for your purposes? 2. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the California Resources Agency (the "CRA")? 3. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from participating in a presentation by the Northern Star Natural Gas Company (the "NSNG") before the state's informal Interagency Liquefied Natural Gas Working Group (the "Working Group")? _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) File No. I-07-004 Page No. 2 4. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (the "FERC") or the U.S. Maritime Administration (the "USMA") in federal licensing proceedings? 5. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the California State Lands Commission (the "CSLC"), the California Coastal Commission (the "CCC"), or the California Public Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") with respect to the Cabrillo Port, Oceanway, Clearwater, or Sound Energy Solutions projects? 6. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with state agencies regarding proposed statutes or regulations of general applicability? 7. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from communicating with the California Energy Commission (the "CEC"), to assist the CEC in reassigning your prior duties, or from communicating with the Attorney General's Office in regards to its gasoline price investigation and legislative proposal pertaining to the sharing of petroleum industry data? 8. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from representing other parties, before federal and state agencies, in proceedings pertaining to the Frontier Project? 9. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from representing parties in rulemaking proceedings before federal or state agencies concerning electric transmission facilities? 10. Do the revolving door provisions prohibit you from requesting information available to the public or from attending public education seminars, public workshops, or public business meetings of the
